Category: --- OWNER / DEVELOPER: the entity that funds and owns the project ---

Description: Company or individual funding a project for profit — develops to sell, lease, or operate. Decision authority on scope, budget, and schedule. Delivers program requirements, design approvals, change authorizations, and substantial completion acceptance. Active across all phases from conception through operations.
Industry Standard: State real estate licensing; SEC (if publicly traded); local development agreements
Common Values: Developer; owner's rep; equity partner; fund manager
Source Systems: Procore (company directory); Yardi; Argus; MRI; Dealpath; Excel
Procore Coverage: Low — tracked in companies table as project owner but no structured classification of owner type
Benchmarking Use: Compare developer decision timelines and change authorization rates across project types
Notes: Role polymorphism: a developer may self-perform CM services. Owner decisions drive cost and schedule more than any other party. Cross-domain: Financial Instruments (funding and change authorization).

Description: Organization building for its own use — corporate campus, hospital, university, government facility. End user and operator of the facility. Defines program requirements, coordinates stakeholders, provides operations input, and plans occupancy. Especially active in Preconstruction and Post-Construction.
Industry Standard: Joint Commission (healthcare); state education codes; GSA (federal); ADA
Common Values: Corporate real estate; healthcare system; university; school district; military
Source Systems: Procore (company directory); Planon; Archibus; CMMS systems; ERP systems
Procore Coverage: Low — tracked as project owner in companies table; no institutional owner classification
Benchmarking Use: Compare program definition completeness and stakeholder coordination timelines across institutional types
Notes: Institutional owners care about lifecycle cost, not just first cost. Internal facility teams often have strong opinions on systems and maintainability.

Description: Federal, state, or local government agency procuring a public project. Regulated procurement authority with statutory constraints — competitive bidding, prevailing wage enforcement, ADA compliance, environmental review. Active across all phases with additional regulatory overlay.
Industry Standard: Miller Act (federal bonding); Davis-Bacon Act (prevailing wage); state procurement statutes; FAR (federal); DBE requirements
Common Values: Federal agency; state DOT; municipal public works; school board; housing authority
Source Systems: Procore (company directory); government procurement portals (SAM.gov, state systems); Excel
Procore Coverage: Low — tracked as project owner; no public entity classification or procurement compliance tracking
Benchmarking Use: Compare public procurement timelines and change order documentation rates vs. private projects
Notes: Procurement rules severely constrain flexibility. Change orders require public documentation. Davis-Bacon prevailing wage on federal projects. Cross-domain: Financial Instruments (public project billing requirements).

Category: --- CONSTRUCTION MANAGEMENT: the entity responsible for delivering the project ---

Description: Company contracted to construct the project — holds risk for cost, schedule, and quality under lump sum or GMP contract. Single point of responsibility to the owner. Manages subcontractors, schedule, quality control, safety, and cost control. Delivers schedule, budget reports, pay applications, daily reports, punchlist management, and closeout documents. Active from Procurement through Closeout (sometimes Preconstruction if CM at Risk).
Industry Standard: AGC contract forms; AIA A201 (general conditions); OSHA 29 CFR 1926; state contractor licensing
Common Values: GC; prime contractor; builder; constructor
Source Systems: Procore (primary user — directory, daily logs, schedule, financials, quality); Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; MS Project; P6
Procore Coverage: High — GC is the primary Procore user; companies, vendors, project_users, and all project management tools serve GC workflows
Benchmarking Use: Compare GC project delivery performance (schedule variance
Notes: cost growth
column10: safety rates) across project types and contract types
column11: The hub of all project information — GC touches every UCDM domain. In lump sum, GC bears cost risk; in GMP, shared savings possible. Cross-domain: all domains.

Element: Construction Manager at Risk (CM at Risk)
Description: CM engaged during design to provide preconstruction services (estimating, VE, constructability, scheduling), then transitions to GC role during construction under a GMP. Most common delivery method for large commercial projects. Early involvement improves cost certainty. Active from Preconstruction through Closeout.
Industry Standard: AIA A133 (CM at Risk); AGC ConsensusDocs 500; state CM licensing
Common Values: CM/GC; CMAR; preconstruction manager → constructor
Source Systems: Procore (primary user — same as GC); Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; estimating tools
Procore Coverage: High — same as GC once in construction; precon estimating tables also available
Benchmarking Use: Compare preconstruction-to-GMP conversion rates and estimate accuracy across CM at Risk projects
Notes: GMP conversion is a key contractual milestone. Pre-construction services agreement → GMP amendment or separate construction contract.

Description: CM acts as owner's agent — manages project but does NOT hold trade contracts or bear construction cost risk. Provides schedule/budget management, trade coordination, quality oversight, and reporting. Owner holds direct contracts with trades. Common on institutional and public projects. Active from Preconstruction through Closeout.
Industry Standard: AIA B132/B133 (CM services); AGC ConsensusDocs 801; CMAA standards
Common Values: CM agent; owner's CM; program manager (single project)
Source Systems: Procore (as project administrator); MS Project; P6; Primavera Unifier; Excel
Procore Coverage: Medium — uses Procore for project management but financial tracking is with individual trade contracts, not CM commitments
Benchmarking Use: Compare CM Agency overhead rates and project delivery outcomes vs. CM at Risk and GC models
Notes: CM does not self-perform and does not hold subcontracts. Owner takes more risk but maintains direct trade relationships.

Description: Single entity responsible for both design and construction under one contract. Single point of responsibility for owner. Manages architect directly and self-coordinates design-construction overlap. Faster delivery due to concurrent design and construction. Active across all phases.
Industry Standard: AIA A141 (design-build); DBIA contract forms; AGC ConsensusDocs 400
Common Values: Design-builder; design-build contractor; EPC contractor
Source Systems: Procore (as GC); BIM 360; Revit (design coordination); Sage 300; Viewpoint
Procore Coverage: High — same as GC for construction phase; design tools outside Procore
Benchmarking Use: Compare design-build delivery speed and cost certainty vs. design-bid-build and CM at Risk
Notes: Owner gives up some design control. Design-builder manages architect directly. Cross-domain: Phases (design and construction overlap significantly).

Description: Firm managing a portfolio of related projects for an owner — multiple projects under one program. Provides portfolio-level schedule, budget, and risk management with standardization across projects. Common for healthcare campuses, university programs, corporate rollouts. Active across all phases on multiple projects simultaneously.
Industry Standard: CMAA (program management standards); PMI PgMP (program management professional)
Common Values: Program manager; portfolio manager; owner's program rep
Source Systems: Procore (multi-project views); Primavera Unifier; Kahua; e-Builder; Oracle P6 (multi-project)
Procore Coverage: Medium — Procore supports multi-project views and portfolio reporting but lacks formal program management workflows
Benchmarking Use: Compare program standardization effectiveness and cross-project consistency metrics
Notes: Provides consistency across projects. Cross-domain: Project Attributes (portfolio-level benchmarking).

Category: --- DESIGN TEAM: the firms that design the project ---

Description: Licensed architecture firm responsible for building design and construction documents — stamp on architectural drawings. Performs construction administration (CA): submittal review, RFI response, field observation, punch list walk. Active from Preconstruction through Closeout. Contracts with owner (design-bid-build/CM) or with design-builder.
Industry Standard: AIA B101 (design services); AIA A201 (general conditions — architect's role); state architecture licensing; NCARB
Common Values: Architect; AOR; design architect; architect of record
Source Systems: Procore (submittals, RFIs, drawings, observations); Revit; AutoCAD; Bluebeam; Newforma; BIM 360
Procore Coverage: Medium — tracked in project_vendors and project_role_memberships (design_team group); submittals and RFIs reference architect as reviewer
Benchmarking Use: Compare architect CA responsiveness (RFI response time
Notes: submittal review duration) across project types
column10: Quality of construction administration varies enormously and directly impacts project quality. Cross-domain: Documents (RFIs, submittals), Phases (Preconstruction through Closeout).

Description: Licensed engineering firm responsible for structural system design — foundation design, connection design, structural calculations. Stamp on structural drawings. Performs submittal review and RFI response for structural scope. Active from Preconstruction through Construction. Typically subconsultant to architect or direct contract with owner.
Industry Standard: ASCE 7 (structural loads); ACI 318 (concrete); AISC 360 (steel); IBC Chapter 16; state PE licensing
Common Values: Structural engineer; EOR (engineer of record); structural consultant
Source Systems: Procore (submittals, RFIs); Revit Structure; ETABS; RAM; RISA; Bluebeam
Procore Coverage: Medium — tracked in project_vendors; submittals and RFIs reference structural engineer as reviewer
Benchmarking Use: Compare structural RFI volume and submittal review turnaround across structural system types
Notes: Shop drawing review is critical — especially steel connections and concrete reinforcing. Special inspections are often driven by structural requirements.

Description: Licensed engineering firm(s) responsible for mechanical, electrical, and plumbing system design — HVAC, plumbing, electrical, fire protection. Energy code compliance, load calculations, equipment schedules. Performs submittal review and RFI response. Active from Preconstruction through Commissioning. Subconsultant to architect or direct owner contract.
Industry Standard: ASHRAE (HVAC design); NEC/NFPA 70 (electrical); IPC/UPC (plumbing); ASHRAE 90.1 (energy); state PE licensing
Common Values: MEP engineer; mechanical engineer; electrical engineer; plumbing engineer
Source Systems: Procore (submittals, RFIs); Revit MEP; AutoCAD MEP; Trane Trace; carrier HAP; Bluebeam
Procore Coverage: Medium — tracked in project_vendors; submittals and RFIs reference MEP as reviewer; no structured engineer-type classification
Benchmarking Use: Compare MEP RFI volume and coordination issue rates across building complexity levels
Notes: MEP may be one firm or multiple specialists. Coordination issues during construction often trace back to design-phase decisions. Cross-domain: Building Elements (HVAC, Plumbing, Electrical).

Description: Licensed engineering firm responsible for site design — grading, utilities, stormwater, roadways, erosion control. Stamp on civil drawings. Active from Preconstruction through Sitework. Subconsultant to architect or direct owner contract.
Industry Standard: ASCE (civil engineering); local stormwater codes; EPA NPDES; ASTM (earthwork); state PE licensing
Common Values: Civil engineer; site engineer; land development engineer
Source Systems: Procore (submittals, RFIs); Civil 3D; AutoCAD; HydroCAD; StormCAD; Bluebeam
Procore Coverage: Low — tracked in project_vendors but civil engineering is primarily pre-construction; limited Procore usage during site design
Benchmarking Use: Compare civil engineering RFI volume and utility conflict rates across site complexity
Notes: Civil work is first constructed and last finished (site improvements). Utility conflicts between civil and structural are common. Cross-domain: Building Elements (Site & Earthwork, Site Utilities).

Description: Licensed engineering firm responsible for fire protection and life safety system design — sprinkler design, fire alarm design, smoke control, egress analysis, fire code compliance. Active from Preconstruction through Commissioning. Subconsultant to architect or MEP engineer, or direct owner contract.
Industry Standard: NFPA 13 (sprinklers); NFPA 72 (fire alarm); NFPA 92 (smoke control); IBC Chapter 9; state PE licensing
Common Values: Fire protection engineer; life safety consultant; FPE
Source Systems: Procore (submittals, RFIs); AutoSprink; Revit; HydraCalc; Bluebeam
Procore Coverage: Low — tracked in project_vendors; fire protection submittals and RFIs exist but engineer type not classified
Benchmarking Use: Compare fire protection RFI volume and fire marshal review pass rates across building types
Notes: Fire protection engineering is increasingly specialized. Smoke control on high-rises and atriums is complex. Fire marshal review is a critical permitting step.

Description: Licensed engineering firm investigating subsurface conditions and recommending foundation design — soil borings, lab testing, foundation recommendations, earthwork specs, dewatering recommendations. Produces the geotechnical report, one of the first project documents. Active from Preconstruction through Foundations. Typically direct contract with owner, engaged before architect.
Industry Standard: ASTM D1586 (SPT); ASTM D2166 (unconfined compression); ASCE (geotechnical); state PE licensing
Common Values: Geotechnical engineer; soils engineer; geotechnical consultant
Source Systems: Procore (document management); gINT (boring logs); geotechnical lab LIMS; Bluebeam
Procore Coverage: Low — geotechnical reports stored in documents but no structured geotechnical data; engaged before Procore typically set up
Benchmarking Use: Compare foundation type recommendations and differing conditions claim rates across geographies
Notes: Foundation type selection (spread footings vs. piles vs. caissons) driven by this report. Differing conditions claims reference it. Cross-domain: Building Elements (Foundations), Phases (Preconstruction).

Description: Licensed design firm responsible for exterior landscape, hardscape, irrigation, and site amenity design. Produces planting plans, irrigation plans, hardscape details. Active during Preconstruction and Site Improvements phase. Subconsultant to architect or direct owner contract.
Industry Standard: ASLA standards; state landscape architecture licensing; SITES (sustainable sites)
Common Values: Landscape architect; LA; landscape designer
Source Systems: Procore (submittals, RFIs); AutoCAD; Land F/X; Revit; Bluebeam
Procore Coverage: Low — tracked in project_vendors; landscape submittals exist but landscape architect not classified as type
Benchmarking Use: Compare landscape scope completion rates and seasonal planting window impacts across climates
Notes: Landscape work is among the last site work completed. Plant material availability and seasonal planting windows affect schedule. Cross-domain: Building Elements (Site Improvements).

Category: --- SPECIALTY CONSULTANTS: firms providing specialized design or advisory services ---

Description: Firm or individual that writes the project manual (specifications) governing materials and installation methods. Product research, code cross-referencing, coordination with design team. Specifications are the legal backbone of construction quality. Active during Preconstruction. Subconsultant to architect or independent contract with owner.
Industry Standard: CSI MasterFormat; CSI SectionFormat; CSI PageFormat; ASTM product standards
Common Values: Spec writer; specifier; project manual author
Source Systems: Procore (specifications module); MasterSpec; SpecLink; BSD; Bluebeam
Procore Coverage: Low — specifications stored in documents but not parsed or structured; spec section references exist on submittals
Benchmarking Use: Compare specification completeness and spec-to-drawing conflict rates (RFI generators) across project types
Notes: Conflicts between specs and drawings generate RFIs. Many architects have in-house spec writers. Cross-domain: Documents (specifications, addenda).

Description: Firm specializing in architectural and functional lighting design — lighting layout, fixture selection, controls design, daylighting analysis, energy code compliance. Bridges engineering and architecture. Active from Preconstruction through Finishes. Subconsultant to architect or electrical engineer.
Industry Standard: IES (Illuminating Engineering Society); ASHRAE 90.1 (energy — lighting power density); ADA (emergency lighting)
Common Values: Lighting designer; lighting consultant; LD
Source Systems: Procore (submittals); Revit; AGi32; DIALux; Bluebeam; lighting manufacturer tools
Procore Coverage: Low — tracked in project_vendors; lighting submittals exist but lighting designer not classified as type
Benchmarking Use: Compare lighting fixture submittal cycle times and controls programming commissioning rates
Notes: LED revolution has changed fixture selection. Controls complexity increasing (DALI, DMX, wireless). Cross-domain: Building Elements (Electrical — Lighting).

Description: Firm specializing in sound isolation, room acoustics, and noise control — STC/IIC ratings, room finish recommendations, mechanical noise criteria, environmental noise assessment. Critical for healthcare, education, residential, and performing arts. Active from Preconstruction through Commissioning. Subconsultant to architect or direct owner contract.
Industry Standard: ASTM E90/E413 (STC); ASTM E492/E989 (IIC); ANSI S12.60 (classroom acoustics); FGI (healthcare)
Common Values: Acoustics consultant; acoustic engineer; vibration consultant
Source Systems: Procore (submittals, observations); acoustic modeling software; sound level meters; Bluebeam
Procore Coverage: None — no acoustics-related tracking in Procore; acoustic test results not captured
Benchmarking Use: Compare acoustic performance compliance rates across occupancy types (healthcare vs. residential vs. education)
Notes: Speech privacy (healthcare), classroom acoustics (education), and impact isolation (residential) are the primary drivers. Cross-domain: Building Elements (Interiors — acoustical elements).

Description: Firm specializing in design and performance of the building exterior skin — air/water/thermal barrier design, curtain wall engineering review, mock-up testing oversight, field quality inspection. Increasingly common as energy codes tighten and envelope litigation grows. Active from Preconstruction through Enclosure. Direct owner contract or subconsultant to architect.
Industry Standard: ASTM E2178/E2357 (air barrier); AAMA 501 (mock-up testing); ABAA standards; energy codes
Common Values: Envelope consultant; building science consultant; waterproofing consultant
Source Systems: Procore (observations, inspections); WUFI (hygrothermal); THERM; Bluebeam; thermal imaging
Procore Coverage: Low — observations and inspections can track envelope issues but no dedicated envelope performance module
Benchmarking Use: Compare envelope deficiency rates and mock-up test pass rates across climate zones
Notes: Often independent from architect for objectivity. Cross-domain: Building Elements (Exterior Enclosure), Phases (Building Enclosure).

Description: Firm managing green building certification and sustainability requirements — LEED/WELL credit tracking, energy modeling coordination, material documentation (EPDs, HPDs, VOC content), commissioning coordination. Active across all phases from design through Commissioning. Direct owner contract or subconsultant to architect.
Industry Standard: LEED (USGBC); WELL (IWBI); SITES; Living Building Challenge; ENERGY STAR; ASHRAE 189.1
Common Values: LEED consultant; sustainability consultant; green building advisor; WELL AP
Source Systems: Procore (document management); ARC (LEED Online); ENERGY STAR Portfolio Manager; Bluebeam
Procore Coverage: Low — no LEED/sustainability tracking module; certification documents stored in project files
Benchmarking Use: Compare LEED certification achievement rates and material documentation compliance across project types
Notes: Material documentation (EPDs, HPDs, VOC content) is a major submittal effort across all trades. Cross-domain: Documents (certification documentation), Phases (Commissioning).

Description: Firm specializing in building code interpretation and life safety compliance — code analysis, occupancy classification, egress analysis, fire resistance requirements, ADA compliance, variance applications. Critical for complex occupancies. Active from Preconstruction through Permitting. Subconsultant to architect or direct owner contract.
Industry Standard: IBC/ICC (building code); NFPA 101 (life safety code); ADA/ICC A117.1; state building codes
Common Values: Code consultant; life safety consultant; code compliance reviewer
Source Systems: Procore (document management); code analysis tools; Bluebeam
Procore Coverage: None — no code compliance tracking in Procore; code analysis stored as documents only
Benchmarking Use: Compare code variance approval rates and permitting timelines for complex occupancies
Notes: Can save months in permitting by facilitating AHJ conversations. Critical for healthcare, high-rise, mixed-use. Cross-domain: Phases (Permitting & Approvals).

Category: --- TRADE CONTRACTORS: firms that perform the physical construction work ---

Description: Specialty contractor performing a specific trade scope of work under contract to the GC — self-performs trade work, manages own workforce, procures trade-specific materials, responsible for quality of installed work and safety of own crews. Typical project has 25-40 subcontractors. Delivers installed work, submittals, daily manpower reports, pay applications, as-built markups, and warranty. Active from Procurement through Closeout. Subcontract with GC (or direct with owner in CM Agency).
Industry Standard: State contractor licensing; OSHA 29 CFR 1926; CSI MasterFormat (scope definition); union agreements (where applicable)
Common Values: Subcontractor; sub; trade contractor; specialty contractor; first-tier sub
Source Systems: Procore (vendors, commitments, pay apps, submittals, daily logs, punch items, observations); Sage 300; Viewpoint; CMiC
Procore Coverage: High — project_vendors table, commitments, pay applications, submittals, daily logs, observations, punch items all track subcontractor activity
Benchmarking Use: Compare subcontractor performance (quality deficiency rates
Notes: safety incident rates
column10: schedule compliance) across trades and project types
column11: The building blocks of construction. Each sub manages their own labor, procurement, and quality. GC coordinates between them. Cross-domain: Resources (Trades), Financial Instruments (Commitments), all quality domains.

Description: GC's own workforce performing specific trade work — typically concrete, carpentry, general conditions, demolition. Same scope as subcontractor but with GC-employed labor. Costs tracked as direct costs rather than commitments. Active from Construction through Closeout.
Industry Standard: State contractor licensing; OSHA 29 CFR 1926; union agreements (where applicable)
Common Values: GC self-perform; in-house crew; direct labor
Source Systems: Procore (direct costs, daily logs — manpower, equipment); Sage 300; Viewpoint; timekeeping systems
Procore Coverage: Medium — daily logs track crew manpower; direct costs track financial; but no formal self-perform vs. sub classification
Benchmarking Use: Compare self-perform vs. subcontracted cost and quality outcomes for common GC trades (concrete
Notes: carpentry)
column10: Self-perform work tracked as direct costs rather than commitments — different financial reporting path. Cross-domain: Financial Instruments (Direct Costs), Resources (Trades).

Category: --- SUPPLIERS & FABRICATORS: firms that provide materials and fabricated products ---

Description: Company that sells construction materials — manufacturer or distributor. Supplies materials per purchase order, schedules deliveries, provides product documentation and warranties. Delivery reliability directly impacts schedule. Active from Procurement through Construction. Purchase order from GC or subcontractor.
Industry Standard: ASTM (material standards); UL (product certification); CSI MasterFormat (material specification sections)
Common Values: Supplier; vendor; distributor; manufacturer rep
Source Systems: Procore (vendors, purchase orders, daily log deliveries); supplier portals; ERP systems; Excel
Procore Coverage: Medium — tracked in project_vendors via purchase orders; daily_log_delivery captures on-site deliveries; no supplier classification
Benchmarking Use: Compare material delivery reliability rates and shortage frequency across material types and suppliers
Notes: Material suppliers tracked via POs, not subcontracts. Material shortages are a top delay cause. Cross-domain: Resources (Materials), Phases (Procurement).

Description: Company that manufactures custom construction components from approved shop drawings — steel fabricators, curtain wall fabricators, precast producers, custom casework shops. Fabrication per approved shop drawings with factory QC. Active from Procurement through Construction. Purchase order or subcontract (if fabricator also installs).
Industry Standard: AISC (steel fabrication); PCI (precast); AAMA (curtain wall); AWI (architectural woodwork)
Common Values: Fabricator; manufacturer; shop; mill; precast plant
Source Systems: Procore (submittals — shop drawings, vendor tracking); fabricator ERP/MRP systems; Tekla; SDS2
Procore Coverage: Medium — tracked in project_vendors; submittals track shop drawing approval; no fabrication progress or factory QC tracking
Benchmarking Use: Compare fabrication lead times and shop drawing resubmittal rates across fabrication types
Notes: Fabrication lead time is often critical path. Factory QC reports are key deliverables. Cross-domain: Phases (Fabrication & Long-Lead Items), Building Elements (by system).

Description: Company providing construction equipment on a rental basis — tower cranes, excavators, aerial lifts, temporary fencing, generators. Equipment mobilization, maintenance, and operator support (if provided). Active during Construction. Rental agreement typically month-to-month or project duration.
Industry Standard: OSHA 29 CFR 1926 Subpart N (cranes); OSHA Subpart L (scaffolding); ANSI/ASME (equipment standards)
Common Values: Equipment rental; crane rental; aerial lift provider; temp power provider
Source Systems: Procore (daily log equipment, vendors); equipment rental portals; fleet management systems; Excel
Procore Coverage: Medium — daily_log_equipment tracks on-site equipment usage; vendors table tracks rental companies; no rental agreement tracking
Benchmarking Use: Compare equipment utilization rates and rental cost per unit across project types and sizes
Notes: Equipment costs are a significant general conditions line item. Tower crane planning dominates logistics on high-rise. Cross-domain: Resources (Equipment), Financial Instruments (Direct Costs).

Category: --- TESTING & INSPECTION: firms that verify quality and compliance ---

Description: Independent testing firm performing code-required special inspections — structural steel connections, concrete testing, fireproofing thickness, welding inspection. Code requires independence from contractor. Active from Construction through Commissioning. Direct contract with owner (required by code).
Industry Standard: IBC Chapter 17 (special inspections); AISC (steel inspection); ACI 318 (concrete inspection); AWS D1.1 (welding)
Common Values: Special inspector; third-party inspector; testing agency
Source Systems: Procore (inspections, observations); testing lab LIMS; inspection reporting tools; Bluebeam
Procore Coverage: Medium — inspections and inspection_items tables track third-party inspections; observations capture findings; no formal agency classification
Benchmarking Use: Compare special inspection deficiency rates and test pass rates across structural system types
Notes: Common inspections: structural connections, concrete cylinders, fireproofing thickness. Cross-domain: Phases (throughout Construction), Building Elements (Structural Frame).

Description: Laboratory that tests construction materials for specification compliance — concrete cylinder testing (7-day and 28-day strength), soil compaction, steel tensile testing, asphalt testing, mortar testing. Active during Construction. Contract with owner, GC, or subcontractor depending on specification.
Industry Standard: ASTM C31/C39 (concrete); ASTM D698/D1557 (compaction); ASTM A370 (steel); ASTM D6927 (asphalt)
Common Values: Testing lab; materials lab; soils lab; concrete testing
Source Systems: Procore (inspections, daily logs); lab LIMS systems; field testing equipment; concrete testing databases
Procore Coverage: Low — inspection hold points can be tracked but test results (break strengths, compaction percentages) are not structured in Procore
Benchmarking Use: Compare concrete cylinder break pass rates and compaction test results across project types and geographies
Notes: Concrete cylinder breaks are the most common test. Failing tests can require coring, load testing, or removal — expensive consequences.

Description: Independent firm verifying building systems perform to design intent — pre-functional testing, functional performance testing, seasonal testing. Produces commissioning plan, checklists, test reports, deficiency lists, final commissioning report. Active from Construction through Post-Construction (seasonal). Direct contract with owner for independence.
Industry Standard: ASHRAE Guideline 0 (commissioning); ASHRAE 202; LEED EAp1; IgCC
Common Values: Commissioning agent; CxA; commissioning authority
Source Systems: Procore (observations, inspections); Cx Alloy; FacilityONE; CxPlanner; BAS systems
Procore Coverage: Low — observations and inspections can informally track commissioning; no dedicated CxA workflow or commissioning module
Benchmarking Use: Compare commissioning deficiency rates and seasonal performance verification outcomes across building types
Notes: LEED and many jurisdictions now require independent CxA. Enhanced commissioning extends to envelope beyond MEP. Cross-domain: Phases (Commissioning), Building Elements (HVAC, Electrical).

Description: Firm monitoring environmental compliance during construction — air quality monitoring, noise monitoring, stormwater sampling, hazmat oversight, regulatory reporting. Required for projects with contaminated soil, asbestos abatement, noise-sensitive neighbors, or stormwater permits. Active during Construction. Contract with owner or GC.
Industry Standard: EPA regulations; NESHAP (asbestos); OSHA 29 CFR 1926.1101 (asbestos); state environmental regulations
Common Values: Environmental consultant; hazmat monitor; industrial hygienist
Source Systems: Procore (daily logs, observations); environmental monitoring instruments; regulatory reporting systems
Procore Coverage: Low — daily logs can capture environmental events; no dedicated environmental monitoring or compliance tracking
Benchmarking Use: Compare environmental compliance incident rates and remediation costs across project types with contamination
Notes: Cross-domain: Phases (Demolition & Site Clearing, Earthwork), Regulatory Authorities (EPA).

Category: --- REGULATORY AUTHORITIES: government entities that approve and inspect ---

Description: Local government agency that reviews plans, issues building permits, performs progress inspections, and issues certificates of occupancy. Permit timeline varies from 2 weeks to 12+ months. Plan review comments generate design revisions. Failed field inspections require correction and re-inspection. Active during Preconstruction (permitting) and Construction through Closeout (CO).
Industry Standard: IBC/ICC (building code); state building codes; local amendments
Common Values: Building department; building inspector; plan reviewer; code official
Source Systems: Procore (inspections); municipal e-permitting portals; permit tracking systems
Procore Coverage: Medium — inspections module can track building department inspections and hold points; no permit tracking
Benchmarking Use: Compare permit approval timelines and inspection pass rates across jurisdictions
Notes: CO is the gate to occupancy. TCO is common when portions remain. Cross-domain: Phases (Permitting & Approvals, Final Inspections).

Description: Fire authority having jurisdiction (AHJ) for fire and life safety compliance — fire protection plan review, fire alarm plan review, field inspections, acceptance testing, hydrant flow testing. Fire marshal sign-off is required for CO. Active from Preconstruction through Commissioning.
Industry Standard: NFPA 1 (fire code); NFPA 13 (sprinklers); NFPA 72 (fire alarm); IBC Chapter 9; state fire codes
Common Values: Fire marshal; fire inspector; AHJ; fire department
Source Systems: Procore (inspections); fire department scheduling systems
Procore Coverage: Medium — inspections track fire marshal visits and acceptance testing; no fire code compliance module
Benchmarking Use: Compare fire marshal inspection pass rates and acceptance testing timelines across building types
Notes: Acceptance testing of fire alarm and sprinkler systems is witnessed by fire marshal. Fire watch may be required during construction. Cross-domain: Building Elements (Fire Protection).

Description: Government agency regulating health-related construction — healthcare facilities, food service, pools, labs, childcare. Plan review for health-related spaces, field inspections, operational permits. Active from Preconstruction through Closeout.
Industry Standard: FGI Guidelines (healthcare); state health codes; FDA (food service); CDC; local health ordinances
Common Values: Health inspector; health department reviewer; environmental health
Source Systems: Procore (inspections); health department portals
Procore Coverage: Low — inspections can track health department visits; no health code compliance tracking
Benchmarking Use: Compare health department inspection requirements and approval timelines across facility types
Notes: Required for hospitals, commercial kitchens, pools, childcare. Adds to closeout timeline.

Description: Federal, state, or local environmental regulatory authority — environmental impact review, stormwater permit oversight, air quality compliance, hazmat oversight. NEPA review on federal projects can add years. Active from Preconstruction through Construction.
Industry Standard: NEPA; CEQA (California); EPA Clean Water Act; Clean Air Act; RCRA (hazardous waste); state environmental laws
Common Values: EPA; state DEQ/DEP; environmental regulator
Source Systems: Procore (daily logs — environmental observations); EPA reporting systems; state environmental portals
Procore Coverage: Low — daily logs can capture environmental compliance events; no environmental permit or compliance tracking
Benchmarking Use: Compare environmental permit timelines and compliance incident rates across project types and geographies
Notes: SWPPP compliance monitored throughout construction. Contaminated site remediation adds significant cost and time. Cross-domain: Phases (Preconstruction, Earthwork).

Description: Federal or state occupational safety and health authority — workplace safety regulation, site inspections (scheduled and unscheduled), citation issuance, fatality investigation. Focus areas: fall protection, scaffolding, excavation, crane operations, struck-by hazards. Active during Construction.
Industry Standard: OSHA 29 CFR 1926 (construction safety); state OSHA plans; ANSI safety standards
Common Values: OSHA; state OSHA; safety inspector; compliance officer
Source Systems: Procore (observations — safety, daily log safety violations); OSHA reporting systems
Procore Coverage: Medium — safety observations and daily_log_safety_violation track safety events; incident reporting exists; no OSHA citation tracking
Benchmarking Use: Compare OSHA recordable incident rates and citation frequencies across project types and GCs
Notes: OSHA can inspect any jobsite. Citations carry financial penalties and can shut down work. Cross-domain: Recurring Violations (insight #11), Resources (Safety Professional).

Description: Local government agency controlling land use, density, setbacks, and project approvals — zoning compliance review, variance review, site plan approval, community review facilitation. Zoning approval is a prerequisite for building permit. Active during Preconstruction.
Industry Standard: Local zoning codes; state planning statutes; APA (American Planning Association) standards
Common Values: Zoning board; planning commission; zoning administrator
Source Systems: Procore (document management); local zoning portals; GIS systems
Procore Coverage: None — no zoning or land use tracking in Procore; zoning documents stored in project files
Benchmarking Use: Compare zoning variance approval rates and conditions-of-approval impact across jurisdictions
Notes: Variances require public hearings. Conditions of approval can significantly affect project scope and cost. Cross-domain: Phases (Permitting & Approvals), Project Attributes (location).

Category: --- FINANCIAL INSTITUTIONS: firms providing financial services and risk transfer ---

Description: Bank or financial institution providing construction financing — construction loan funding, draw review, progress inspection (lender's inspector), compliance monitoring. Lender draws tied to percent complete — monthly pay applications scrutinized. Active across all phases (funding drives everything). Loan agreement with owner/developer.
Industry Standard: OCC (banking regulation); Uniform Commercial Code; state lending laws
Common Values: Construction lender; bank; financial institution; loan officer
Source Systems: Procore (pay applications — lender reviews); banking systems; draw management platforms (Built Technologies, Rabbet)
Procore Coverage: Low — pay applications exist and are reviewed by lenders, but no lender-specific workflow or draw tracking
Benchmarking Use: Compare construction loan draw frequency and cost overrun trigger rates across project types and sizes
Notes: Cost overruns can trigger loan covenant issues. Lender's inspector verifies progress independently. Cross-domain: Financial Instruments (Pay Applications, Retainage).

Description: Insurance company issuing performance and payment bonds guaranteeing contractor's obligations. Bonds protect owner (performance bond) and subcontractors (payment bond). Public projects require bonds by law (Miller Act federal, Little Miller Act state). Active from Procurement through Closeout. Bond agreement with GC.
Industry Standard: Miller Act (federal bonding); Little Miller Act (state); state surety regulations
Common Values: Surety; bonding company; performance bond; payment bond
Source Systems: Procore (vendor insurance tracking); surety bond portals; contractor prequalification systems
Procore Coverage: Low — company_vendor_insurances and project_vendor_insurances track insurance certificates; no bond-specific tracking
Benchmarking Use: Compare bond claim rates and contractor default frequencies across project types and sizes
Notes: Bond capacity limits how much work a GC can take on. Consent of surety required for final payment. Cross-domain: Financial Instruments (Contract Closeout).

Description: Companies providing construction insurance — general liability, workers' comp, builders risk, professional liability, umbrella, OCIP/CCIP wrap-up programs. Certificate of insurance collection is a major administrative task. Active across all phases. Insurance policies held by each project party.
Industry Standard: State insurance regulations; ISO (Insurance Services Office); NCCI (workers' comp classification)
Common Values: Insurance broker; carrier; GL; WC; builders risk; OCIP; CCIP
Source Systems: Procore (vendor insurance certificates); insurance certificate management (PINS, myCOI, Evident); Excel
Procore Coverage: Medium — company_vendor_insurances and project_vendor_insurances track certificates of insurance; insurance compliance monitoring available
Benchmarking Use: Compare insurance compliance rates and builders risk claim frequencies across project types
Notes: Wrap-up (OCIP/CCIP) programs consolidate insurance under one policy. Builders risk covers property damage during construction.

Category: --- UTILITIES: service providers for permanent building infrastructure ---

Description: Local electric utility company providing permanent electrical service — service design review, transformer supply/placement, metering, permanent connection, energization. Utility coordination starts early — transformer location, service size, and meter configuration affect design. Active during Preconstruction (service design) and Construction (connection). Permanent power energization is a major milestone.
Industry Standard: NEC/NFPA 70 (service entrance); local utility standards; state PUC regulations
Common Values: Electric utility; power company; utility provider
Source Systems: Procore (daily logs, RFIs); utility company portals; design coordination tools
Procore Coverage: Low — tracked in project_vendors; RFIs may reference utility coordination; no utility service tracking
Benchmarking Use: Compare utility coordination timelines and permanent power energization milestone achievement rates
Notes: Transformer location and service size affect design. Permanent power energization eliminates temporary power cost. Cross-domain: Phases (Electrical Rough-In), Building Elements (Electrical).

Description: Local gas utility providing natural gas service — service design review, gas main extension (if required), meter installation, service connection. Gas service for HVAC, domestic hot water, commercial kitchen. Active during Preconstruction and Construction. Service connection typically later in construction.
Industry Standard: NFPA 54 (National Fuel Gas Code); local utility standards; state PUC regulations
Common Values: Gas utility; gas company; gas service provider
Source Systems: Procore (RFIs, daily logs); utility company portals
Procore Coverage: Low — tracked in project_vendors if involved; no gas service tracking
Benchmarking Use: Compare gas service connection timelines and main extension requirements across project locations
Notes: Gas service affects HVAC system type and kitchen design. Cross-domain: Building Elements (HVAC, Plumbing).

Description: Municipal authority providing domestic water and sanitary sewer service — service size determination, tap/connection permits, meter installation, water quality testing. Water service affects fire protection (hydrant flow, fire pump supply). Active during Preconstruction and Construction. Sanitary sewer capacity can limit project density.
Industry Standard: International Plumbing Code; AWWA (water works standards); local utility standards; EPA Clean Water Act
Common Values: Water authority; sewer authority; water district; municipal utility
Source Systems: Procore (RFIs, inspections, daily logs); utility company portals
Procore Coverage: Low — tracked in project_vendors if involved; inspections may capture utility connection approvals; no water/sewer service tracking
Benchmarking Use: Compare water service connection timelines and tap fee costs across jurisdictions
Notes: Sanitary sewer capacity can limit project density. Fire protection water supply (hydrant flow) is a design driver. Cross-domain: Building Elements (Plumbing, Site Utilities).

Description: Companies providing data, voice, and internet service — service design, conduit and fiber routing, equipment room requirements, service activation. Multiple carriers may serve the building. Active during Preconstruction and Construction. Service agreement may require conduit and room provision by contractor.
Industry Standard: TIA-568 (cabling standards); TIA-569 (pathways); local franchise agreements
Common Values: Telecom provider; ISP; carrier; fiber provider; cable company
Source Systems: Procore (RFIs, daily logs); carrier portals; telecom design tools
Procore Coverage: Low — tracked in project_vendors if involved; no telecom service tracking
Benchmarking Use: Compare telecom infrastructure coordination timelines and multi-carrier provisioning rates
Notes: Conduit routing and telecom room sizing are design-phase decisions. Fiber-to-building availability varies by location. Cross-domain: Building Elements (Low Voltage & Technology).

Category: --- STANDARD MEASURES: KPIs and metrics derived from organizational performance tracking ---

Description: Total number of distinct subcontractors and suppliers engaged on a project — measured from awarded subcontracts and purchase orders. Indicates project complexity and coordination burden.
Industry Standard: AGC (project complexity metrics); PMI PMBOK
Common Values: Typical range: 15-25 (small), 25-40 (mid), 40-80 (large/complex)
Source Systems: Procore (project_vendors, commitments); Sage 300; Viewpoint; CMiC
Procore Coverage: High — project_vendors table provides direct count of engaged vendors per project
Benchmarking Use: Compare subcontractor density across project types and value bands to benchmark coordination complexity
Notes: More subs = more coordination interfaces. Projects with 50+ subs have exponentially more coordination challenges.

Description: Percentage of project scope covered by contracted subcontractors vs. remaining unawarded scope — awarded value / estimated total value × 100. Tracks buyout progress during procurement.
Industry Standard: AGC (procurement metrics); AACE
Common Values: Expressed as percentage; target: 80-90% before mobilization; 95%+ by mid-construction
Source Systems: Procore (commitments vs. budget); Sage 300; Viewpoint; CMiC
Procore Coverage: High — commitments and budget modules enable buyout tracking; committed cost vs. estimated cost
Benchmarking Use: Compare buyout completion rates and timelines across project types and delivery methods
Notes: Buyout gaps late in construction create budget risk. Cross-domain: Phases (Procurement — Bidding & Buyout), Financial Instruments (Commitments).

Description: Percentage of project vendors with current, compliant insurance certificates on file — compliant vendors / total vendors × 100. Tracks risk exposure from uninsured or underinsured trades.
Industry Standard: AIA A201 (insurance requirements); state insurance requirements
Common Values: Expressed as percentage; target: 100%; typical: 85-95%
Source Systems: Procore (vendor insurance tracking); myCOI; PINS; Evident; Excel
Procore Coverage: High — company_vendor_insurances and project_vendor_insurances track certificate status and expiration
Benchmarking Use: Compare insurance compliance rates across project types and identify chronically non-compliant vendor categories
Notes: Insurance compliance is a contractual requirement. Non-compliant vendors create liability risk. Cross-domain: Financial Institutions (Insurance Broker).

Description: OSHA recordable incident rate per subcontractor — (number of recordable incidents × 200,000) / hours worked. Enables comparison of safety performance across trades and companies.
Industry Standard: OSHA 29 CFR 1904 (recordkeeping); EMR (Experience Modification Rate); TRIR (Total Recordable Incident Rate)
Common Values: TRIR typical: 0.5-3.0 (varies by trade); EMR typical: 0.7-1.3
Source Systems: Procore (observations — safety, incidents); safety management systems; OSHA logs
Procore Coverage: Medium — safety observations and incidents track events per vendor_id; hours worked not captured, limiting rate calculation
Benchmarking Use: Compare safety incident rates by trade and subcontractor to identify high-risk vendors across projects
Notes: Cross-domain: Recurring Violations (insight #11 — safety observation patterns by vendor), Resources (Trades).

Description: Average days for design team members to respond to RFIs and review submittals — measured from RFI creation to response and submittal submission to review completion. Indicates design team engagement quality.
Industry Standard: AIA A201 (RFI and submittal timelines); project-specific response requirements
Common Values: RFI response: typical 7-14 days contractual; actual 10-21 days avg. Submittal review: typical 14-21 days; actual 14-30 days
Source Systems: Procore (rfis, submittals — timestamps for creation, response, review); BIM 360; Newforma
Procore Coverage: High — rfis and submittals tables have creation, due, and response timestamps enabling cycle time calculation
Benchmarking Use: Compare architect and engineer responsiveness across project types to identify review bottlenecks
Notes: Slow RFI response cascades into schedule delays. Submittal review delays cascade into procurement and fabrication. Cross-domain: Documents (RFIs, Submittals), Phases (Procurement).

Element: Change Order Dispute Rate by Trade
Description: Percentage of change orders disputed or rejected by trade — disputed COs / total COs per trade × 100. Identifies which trade relationships generate the most commercial friction.
Industry Standard: AIA A201 (change order process); AGC contract forms
Common Values: Expressed as percentage; typical: 5-15% disputed; varies significantly by trade and contract type
Source Systems: Procore (change_events, commitment_change_orders — status fields); Sage 300; Viewpoint; CMiC
Procore Coverage: High — change_events and commitment_change_orders with status tracking enable dispute rate calculation per vendor
Benchmarking Use: Compare change order dispute rates by trade across project types and contract types
Notes: High dispute rates indicate scope definition problems or adversarial relationships. Cross-domain: Financial Instruments (Change Management), Resources (Trades).
